summary:
The composition of dentin includes significant amounts of hydroxylapatite requiring specific conditions for proper formation; odontoblast activity drives this process with localized controls via ions such as phosphate crucially involved but not detailed regarding unique phosphorylations per se among constituent proteins directly marking them out distinctly from other tissues or structures without further context linking those exact molecules' modifications back solely to dentinal status alone based strictly upon current information available here.
To identify if any particular phosphoryl group indicates dentin presence more definitively would need additional research focusing precisely on identifying characteristic post-translational modification patterns within known dental-specific biomolecules under varying physiological contexts compared against non-dental counterparts where similar markers might overlap less frequently thus providing clearer diagnostic utility when present exclusively alongside typical histological features observed macroscopically/structurally consistent across multiple studies reviewed herein pointing towards general importance yet lacking specificity pinpointed exactly onto single molecular level changes noted above due limitations inherent only reviewing secondary literature sources compiled initially now concluding my analysis accordingly while acknowledging potential gaps remaining unexplored beyond scope addressed today concerning direct correlation between certain defined phosphorylations versus definitive identification criteria established elsewhere potentially existing outside our immediate purview currently discussed amongst experts working closely related fields
